speaker: interviewer
text: I say Tape 2, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 2 of the interview with Marian Horn. I am Rivka Wakslak. Today is September 13, 1995. You were telling me about Samobor. You had come to there from Trieste.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We came to Samobor and there was a small farming community. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went to school there for a few months. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I spoke fluent Yugoslav at that point because of continuous trips to Yugoslavia. And I guess, like most European children, languages are not a problem and you learn very quickly. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I enjoyed speaking with the peasants in the area that were beautifully dressed in their costumes on Sundays, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very friendly people, very, very nice people. One day, I was in the house. And we had a rather large bay window, or it seemed like a bay window. I know I was able to sit on top of the window and look out. And I-- I don't know the date. I don't know exactly what time of the year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how old you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must have been about eight and a half or nine, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about nine. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I saw this motorcycle pulling up to the house into the driveway with a sidecar. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I believe that Yugoslavia was in the hands of the Germans at that point. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I knew that the Croatians immediately gave up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Samobor was a part of Croatia.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was part of Croatia. So was Zagreb. They gave up immediately. And there was a-- like the Ger-- like the Yugoslav Gestapo was the Ustase. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took over. And they were-- from what I remember-- and this is very timely because of the times that we're going through right now-- is the-- they were persecuting the Serbs, who were fighting to surrender to the Germans [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very vigorously, right from the beginning. But coming back, these two men came in uniform, black uniforms-- black leather coats and boots and black hats-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] literally sent chills through me at this point because I knew exactly what was happening. They came out and very politely approached, rang the doorbell, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] asked for my father. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they identified themselves, that they were from the SS, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that within three days we should be at the railroad station in Zagreb. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's basically the beginning of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents-- did they talk to other people? Were there other Jews in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. There were no other Jews in the area. No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, basically, through my growing years, almost-- from very rare occasions, I was the only Jew in many, many places. So it was just the nature of the-- because we never lived in a ghetto or, you know, in an environment where we were living with a homogeneous group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents discuss it with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they discussed it with us-- with us children. And they said, look, you know, you're going to have to pack some things, and, you know, make it the important things. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we have to report. We don't know what the future will hold. We don't know where we're going, but we're going to try our very best to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father had this reassuring quality about him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We said, OK, you know? And I remember we went to the-- to Zagreb. We took the train from Samobor to Zagreb. And there we were gathered. And there were a lot of other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were put on the train. The train was not a cattle train. It was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would imagine a third-class train with baggage areas, from what I remember. And we traveled for several hours. And we arrived at this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place. It had wide open spaces. It had several tremendous haylofts. But it was like a two-story structure. The lower structure was cement or concrete, with bars on the windows. And the upper one was a tremendous loft. We were told that that's where we're going to stay and to gather some straw. And there were rows of people lining up straw and for sleeping quarters. I found out afterwards that there were political prisoners in the lower area where the bars-- iron bars-- were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Kerestinec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I believe it's K-E-R-E-S-T-N-E-Z. The-- when we arrived there, there was a very large table where everybody was checked in. Their names and vital information was submitted. And it was both Germans and the Ustase. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the people were told that whatever possessions you have, you have to surrender it at this point. So my father cornered me. And I remember I had on a little smock dress. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he took me in a corner and he stuffed my panties with money. And he said, don't let anybody-- you have to hide this. This might be our only salvation. I hate to put the responsibility on you, but hide it. And make sure you remember where you hide it and that nobody sees you. I remember the-- the trauma was unbelievable. I just-- streaming tears. I didn't know what to do. And I rem-- I-- I-- I knew the importance of this, that this was imperative that I couldn't be caught. And I found a place. I hid it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was out in the fields near an old chicken coop. And I was very careful because there were guards around. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I managed to-- to hide it. And I witnessed such a sad thing. There was a man who was also hiding something. And he took a pocket knife that he had or something, an object. And he cut a piece of grass out of the ground and hid it underneath. And lo and behold, shortly after that, a cow came along because there was cattle on that-- it was a very large area-- picked up the piece of grass and ate the grass with whatever was underneath it. So it was heartbreaking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This place was infested with rats. But when I'm-- I mean, the rats were like small dogs. And they ate everything and anything. So whatever belongings we had or suitcases, they just destroyed everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father arranged that the young people would patrol at night from our own group. And I think at that point, we were a group of about 280 people-- 280 or 286 people, children, elderly people, younger people, boys, girls. It was a mix, families, in essence. Did this group stay together for a long period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- well, whoever survived with time. At that point, we were a group. And it was a very mixed group of people. There were a lot of Hungarians, Czech, Austrians, a few Yugoslav Jews. It was a very, again, eclectic group of people with different backgrounds-- some religious that I had very bad experiences with. I hate to say that, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soap was one of the most important commodities. It was-- food was secondary, believe it or not. Soap, a piece of soap, was one of the most important things in order to maintain your hygiene and be able to wash a few little things. And I remember going to the area where they had put the possessions of the people. At that point, it was in the higher loft.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were a couple people who were praying, religious. And then I caught them stealing soap from people. And it left a very lasting impression. And I-- I was a little kid, but outspoken. And I said, you know, I thought I saw you praying. But yet you were stealing. And we're all in the same boat. Stealing from other people, where is your explanation for this? They couldn't give me an explanation. Very heavy stuff. They gave you food, the Ustase, the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Whatever was growing we were able to gather. And there was-- there was no such thing as food, per se. It was the husk of corn-- not the kernels of the corn, the husk of corn-- which some people were able to mash and make something out of it. And we all broke out in carbuncles. Uh, that was a really big problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Roughly speaking, about six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't provide regular foodstuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They-- they-- we were able to pick what was growing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody was allowed a pitcher of water a day. And from the pitcher of water, you were able to do what you wanted to do. So there was drinking water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother taught us that you brush your teeth, you wash your face, you wash your torso, you wash your bottom part of your body. And the last thing you use it for is to wash your feet. And then whatever water was there, you wash out your panties or underwear. Didn't leave much for drinking.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hygiene came first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the thing is this. You-- you-- you measured. You divided. And you used, you know, a small portion or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able-- was your mother able to cook anything in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --just field gathering.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was just everybody cooked-- the people-- it was not organized. It was not, you know, like the Germans had in the-- it was not an organized camp in that sense. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: The guards in charge were Ustase?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Ustase, predominantly Ustase. Um, some peo--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat the prisoners well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, people were beaten. People were shot. People tried to-- anybody tried to make a getaway, they took a count every morning if somebody was missing. And I remember there was one incident where the son of somebody who was there who escaped. And the next day, the father was shot. And they made it a very public thing, so that, you know, you shouldn't get any ideas.

speaker: interviewer
text: You regularly witnessed people being shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That was a common occurrence, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being really beaten with nightsticks. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And the rats by the hundreds. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember one night going to sleep on the straw. We had these rows. And I remember I thought it was very funny. This woman let out a yelp. She had hung up something resembling a robe, you know, that she took along with her. And she hung it up on the nail. And she was going to the outhouse. And she put her hand in her pocket, and one of the rats had given birth in her pocket. And we, as kids, thought that was very funny. So, you know, there's humor. When you're looking for humor, there's humor. Same thing across from where I was sleeping, there was a man who hung up his underwear. And he had pink underwear. I don't know why I remember that, but I thought that was very funny, somebody having-- a man having pink underwear. [LAUGHS] So you look for humor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you occupy your time during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know. I do know that my father spent time with both my brother and I [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with language, geography, and arithmetic, and having us memorize words and spelling words and because he felt that we come out of this, your brains cannot turn to mush. And on the same token, my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had nothing to eat. I mean, there was really-- there was-- there was hardly any water. The bread was one slice of cornbread a day, whatever mush was made-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But I don't-- I don't-- there was no butter. There was no-- I mean, there was nothing. There was nothing. That was not my major problem somehow. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It-- it does not stick in my mind as being the major, major thing in my-- in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I remember my mother having-- there was a crate and she covered with-- with something. And we had this slop. [LAUGHS] And my brother put his elbow on the crate. And she gave him a smack. [LAUGHS] Off with the elbow. She said, just remember, children, if we come out of this alive, you're going to be a lady, and you're going to be a gentleman. So no matter what, mind your table manners. And we laughed. We said, what table manners? [LAUGHS] But she was right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there. [SIGHS] All I know is that every day there was a patrol, and they used to spear the rats. And then they were put into a heap. And then the Ustase used to pour gasoline over it, or, you know, petroleum or whatever, and set that on fire because that was the only way to have some kind of control. But they were so tame, these rats, that you could-- you could literally spear them. And they were by the hundreds, by the hundreds. Every night, there was a bonfire with rats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents put to work during the day in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes. But I'm not-- I think this was basically such a emotional period and such a-- an unbelievable change in my life that I'm vague about certain things. I remember that one morning we were told we're going to be transported somewhere else, and, you know, everybody should be ready. And we were on transport trains for several days to the southern part of Yugoslavia, which-- Herzegovina. And we were going to Sarajevo. And en route, we found out-- maybe that would give us some chronology-- that the United States had gone to war with Japan.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then, on the way, the train stopped. It was not a regular train station. It just stopped. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the-- a couple Ustases came and told us that there was a interesting incident going on and it would be very educational for all of us to see. And we witnessed a woman [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] being gang-raped, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the man watching and being beaten while he was watching. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Men around laughing, and then being told, yelled at, something. And they were digging a hole in the ground. They were given shovels. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when that task was completed, the man was shot in the head, and he was pushed into the hole. And then the woman was pushed in the hole after him. And the dirt was just covering her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We found out that this was a Serb partisan who was found and captured. And that was his wife. And if anybody wonders about the hatred between the Croatians and the-- and the Serbs, this is a small example of it. It was absolutely one of the most atrocious experiences to watch. I had nightmares about this incident for I don't know how long. It was a horror, absolute horror. The inhumanity of-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was so realistic. It was-- it was-- unreal. Being-- seeing people shot was bad enough. Seeing people being beaten to a pulp. But this was just-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was just unbelievable. And then we went on. [SIGHS] Train went on. You heard-- you had your entertainment for the day. Now we go on. And we went to Sarajevo. In Sarajevo, the train stopped. And that went into a much more organized camp. And there must have been several thousands of people, all different kinds of people. They segregated this group that came together into one area. And there, there was food. They had housing of sorts. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was more organized. It was-- you could say it was a camp.
